for the ikan bakar part, i think it is better you go for restaurant that serve the actual ikan bakar. i would recommend anywhere in kuala perlis, located in the state of perlis. it is like 6 hours drive from kl though but i guarantee you it is worth it. they only serve the dishes during night, so you will need to get yourself somewhere to stay for the night. in respective order, get yourself anything between siakap (asian sea bass), jenahak (snapper), or gerut (saddle grunt) with the gerut being the best among them all. you will want to ask for 'masak santan' which is coconut cream gravy on top of the fish. i am just grateful to be born a malaysian, get to taste those coconut cream gravy on a gerut, what a fascinating meal ever created!

as a penang chinese guy, three dishes that are on top of everything, they are keow teow teng (pork soup keow teow), wantan mee (pork mince wantan with egg noodle), and the last one is char keow teow.they are the three kings.then later there are hokkien mee (prawn soup noodle), asam laksa and curry noodle, they are the three dukes.the the less famous but also delicious such as keow chap (pork, duck, chicken based soup noodles), jawa mee (tomato soup noodle), char tua pan (wok fried big sizes noodle served with gravy soup, pork and prawn based food). these are the three counts. the remaining rest as above than average food from chinese.as for malays and indians food, they are colourful and coconut milk based, and very tasty.so, after all delicious penang food, maybe your body become so hot, you can drink green coconut water too. ahha

i feel like the nasi lemak at the start is not just good cooking but more importantly genius packaging/marketing, by stacking the one side dish on top of the rice, wrapped beautifully in a banana leaf with sambal slathered on it. something tells me it’s not that special in terms of flavour. in most places that would be insufficient for nasi lemak, esp with just ikan bilis, egg, sambal, rice, but they made it look like a good quantity with genius packaging. in other places, the nasi lemak, sambal, and side dishes are served separately, so you can easily tell when the ingredients are few.my fav nasi lemak place has rice, sambal, eggs, chicken, prawns, and otah. some say it’s too much but not necessarily if the chef knows how to complement everything together. and no, the side dishes are all served separately (on the same plate but not stacked into a ‘ball’ like the video’s one) because the side dishes are tasty enough to eat on their own.

mark…!! please eat nasi kandar as simple as possible.. nasi kandar always eat with chicken tight..and dont take a lot of protein…tips please do this step..if you eat nasi kandar..- nasi putih or nasi dalca- kuah banjir - chicken tight ( ayam goreng tight or ayam bawang tight or ayam tanjung tight )- telur masin ( salted egg ) the bigger the eggs york the better- telur bawang ( omelette )- kobis (cabbage)- bendi (lady finger)- sambal nyok ( coconut scradded sambal )- bawang goreng ( fried onion ) must havethis is local choice..try this step just change protein..hope you you enjoy nasi kandar..

penang is my favorite place for asian food. my hotel had the best fruit breakfast that got me going until lunch, then i just had to eat solid food! seafood for both lunch and dinner.. and for my late snack, was my preference. there is a food court that i ate at, on my first day that i could not find again. it was pure white, and huge. white tent roof, white tables, white chairs, white restaurants. very clean, too. there were about 30 different food vendors. i'm going to add that everyone eating there was dressed to the 9's, not poor. it was the best food i'd had in malaysia anywhere. i got to eat at the 8 story mall on the beach on penang island, and it was good, but not anywhere near the same as the street food at the big white outdoor food court food. i need to go visit again, just for the food.

wow! blondie finally in malaysia! so exciting!! welcome!! 欢迎 😃😃👏👏actually, there is no such thing as pure __ (whatever race) food here. you would find a mixture of hybrid fusion of different influences in all these food. you have malay and india influences in the local chinese food; chinese influences in both malay or indian food etc.. it's just one huge melting pot of food cultures heaven here. btw, i think the roti tsunami is a bit of an overkill. i personally prefer the traditional, conventional way - just the bread with the sauce.. you would find that malaysians like this kind of crazy stuff- to "flood" their food with different sauces, and to overflow their plate with all sort of combo.. it's kind of messy and not necessarily the best way to enjoying the food. but then again, people are free to eat in whatever way they enjoy the best. and you must have those huge blood clams (si ham) in the char kuey teow!! anyway, have a great trip and hope you enjoy the food here (and come again!) 😉🙂🙂

you’ve got the origins of chicken rice a bit mixed up. it was the hainanese migrants who brought the dish from hainan to singapore, malaysia, and other places. they arrived later than other chinese groups, such as those from quanzhou, chaoshan, and other coastal areas. by the time the hainanese came, the coolie trade was already saturated, and opportunities in commerce—like the tea and chinese goods trade—were limited. as a result, many of them found work as cooks for the british colonial households. this eventually led them to venture into the food and beverage industry.their entry into this space gave rise to several uniquely hainanese culinary creations that are distinct to singapore and malaysia. among them was their localized version of wenchang chicken, adapted using local poultry and ingredients. naturally, the dish evolved differently in various regions due to the availability of ingredients and local preferences.
